text: The laparoscopic view shows the trocar puncturing the large mucinous cystadenoma on the right side, causing the cyst to rupture. The surgeon aspirates the spilled contents, which appear slimy and sticky due to the mucin content.

The surgeon visualizes the opening created by the trocar insertion over the cyst. The initial step involves aspirating the mucinous fluid from the abdominal cavity by retracting the cyst and aspirating fluid from the paracolic gutters. The mucinous cystadenoma is noted to be multiloculated, indicating multiple small loculi that may require aspiration.

The surgeon uses three ports, with two lateral and one central above the suprapubic region, to perform the cystectomy. The ovarian cortex is cut open along the anti-mesenteric border of the ovary, reducing blood loss and facilitating the procedure. The shearer is used to separate the thick ovarian cortex while gently performing the cystectomy, maintaining the ovarian architecture for potential suturing.

The surgeon uses gentle traction and counter traction to perform meticulous cystectomy on a large cyst, coagulating any tiny bleeders to maintain a clean surgical field. The three-port technique allows for the insertion of needle holders through two lateral ports, providing strong counter traction during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies and projects the remaining loculi of the mucinous cyst, aiming to remove them intact. Dense adhesions between the cyst and the ovarian cortex are visible, particularly at the hilar region. Gentle traction and counter traction are applied to avoid shearing venous connections and excessive bleeding.

The surgeon has successfully enucleated the entire mucinous cyst intact, preserving the ovarian cortex. Minimal blood loss is observed due to adherence to the dissection plane and coagulation of small blood vessels. The cystectomy is complete, and the specimen is prepared for removal from the peritoneal cavity using an endobag. The endobag is introduced into the pelvic cavity, and the specimen is placed inside. Needle holders provide stability and traction, while scissors from the central port ribbonize the specimen for extraction through a ten mm port.

The surgeon thoroughly washes the peritoneal cavity with five percent dextrose normal saline, infiltrating approximately three to five liters to act as a mucolytic agent. Any remaining mucoid collection from the cyst rupture is aspirated out. The ovarian volume is observed returning to normal size and is placed in the pouch of Douglas. The cystectomy bed is visible without any bleeding.
